ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a Chinese flowering cabbage food product entry; the blanched preparation variant is not separately coded, so closest_parent is used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 codes the raw vegetable; the blanched dish preparation is not separately enumerated. Closest parent used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT concept found for blanched choy sum as a prepared dish.

preparation_method: drizzled with oyster sauce or light soy sauce
main_ingredients: oyster sauce or light soy sauce
allergens: soy (from soy sauce or oyster sauce)

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard plate of blanched choy sum as served in a Singapore coffeeshop or Chinese restaurant, approximately 150 g including sauce drizzle.

nutrient_source.title: Cai Xin (Chinese Flowering Cabbage), raw — HPB Singapore Foodo / USDA FoodData Central Brassica rapa subsp. chinensis adapted for blanched preparation
nutrient_source.organization: Health Promotion Board Singapore (HPB) / USDA FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: HPB Foodo lists choy sum (菜心) as a vegetable dish. Nutrient values for blanched choy sum are adapted from HPB Singapore nutrient data for cooked choy sum and cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central entry for Brassica rapa subsp. chinensis (cooked, boiled). Blanching causes minor leaching of water-soluble nutrients; values reflect cooked state. Sodium reflects a light oyster sauce or soy sauce drizzle typical of the dish as served.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of non-starchy leafy green vegetables — analogous estimate based on cooked Brassica vegetables
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI entry exists for blanched choy sum. GI estimated at 15 based on University of Sydney GI Database values for cooked non-starchy Brassica vegetables (e.g., boiled cabbage GI ~10, boiled broccoli GI ~10–15). A conservative estimate of 15 is used. GL per 150 g serving = (15 × 2.8 × 1.5) / 100 = 0.63, rounded to 0.6. This is a very low GL consistent with non-starchy leafy greens.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium from oyster sauce or soy sauce drizzle
hypertension.guidance: Blanched choy sum is inherently low in sodium, but the oyster sauce or soy sauce drizzle typically added at service can contribute 200–350 mg sodium per 100 g; individuals with hypertension should request reduced sauce or substitute with a light low-sodium soy sauce drizzle.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low glycemic index and low carbohydrate content
type2_diabetes.guidance: With a GI of approximately 15 and only 2.8 g carbohydrate per 100 g, blanched choy sum has a negligible glycemic load and is an excellent vegetable choice for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes, supporting blood glucose control when consumed as part of a balanced meal.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Low saturated fat, high dietary fibre, and micronutrient density
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Blanched choy sum is very low in saturated fat (0.1 g/100 g), provides dietary fibre (1.8 g/100 g), folate, potassium, and vitamin C, all of which support cardiovascular health; the primary concern is the sodium contributed by the sauce, which should be minimised for individuals with cardiovascular disease.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Blanched choy sum (白灼菜心, Bai Zhuo Cai Xin) is a classic Cantonese and Singaporean Chinese vegetable dish made from choy sum (Brassica rapa var. parachinensis), also known as Chinese flowering cabbage or cai xin. The vegetable is briefly blanched in boiling water until just tender-crisp, then drained and plated. It is typically finished with a drizzle of oyster sauce or light soy sauce, a few drops of sesame oil, and sometimes fried garlic or shallots. The result is a vibrant green dish with a mild, slightly sweet, and faintly bitter flavour, with a tender yet slightly crunchy texture. Blanched choy sum is a staple side dish in Chinese coffeeshops (kopitiams), zi char restaurants, and dim sum establishments across Singapore, Hong Kong, and southern China. It is one of the most commonly ordered vegetable dishes in Singapore's hawker and restaurant culture. Nutritionally, it is very low in calories (approximately 22 kcal per 100 g), low in carbohydrates (2.8 g/100 g), and provides useful amounts of dietary fibre, folate, vitamin C, vitamin A, calcium, and iron. Its glycemic index is very low (estimated ~15), making it suitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes or metabolic syndrome. The primary nutritional concern is the sodium contributed by the sauce drizzle, which is relevant for individuals with hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The dish is naturally vegetarian when prepared with soy sauce instead of oyster sauce, and is dairy-free and gluten-free when prepared with tamari.
common_user_queries: How many calories in 白灼菜心?
Is choy sum good for diabetics?
What is cai xin in English?
How is 白灼菜心 prepared?
What sauce is used on blanched choy sum?
Can hypertension patients eat blanched choy sum?
What is the difference between choy sum and bok choy?

confidence_reason: HPB Singapore Foodo lists choy sum as a vegetable food item and provides nutrient data for cooked choy sum. Values have been adapted for the blanched preparation with sauce drizzle. No single exact HPB entry for 白灼菜心 as a complete dish was confirmed; values are cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central for cooked Brassica rapa. GI is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database values for cooked non-starchy Brassica vegetables. Confidence is medium rather than high due to the adapted rather than exact source match.
notes: Sodium value of 280 mg/100 g reflects the dish as typically served with a light oyster sauce or soy sauce drizzle; the vegetable itself blanched in plain water would have approximately 20–30 mg sodium/100 g. Vitamin B12 is 0.0 as this is a plant-based vegetable dish that naturally contains no vitamin B12 (naturally zero). Vitamin D is 0.0 as choy sum is a leafy green vegetable with negligible vitamin D content (naturally zero). Trans fat is 0.0 as no hydrogenated fats are used in this preparation (naturally zero). Cholesterol is 0.0 as this is a plant-based dish (naturally zero). monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are estimated from HPB and USDA sources for cooked choy sum. GI set to 15 as an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database values for cooked non-starchy Brassica vegetables; no direct GI measurement for blanched choy sum was found in the approved GI database. Dietary flags: is_vegan set to false because the standard preparation uses oyster sauce; a vegan variant using soy sauce exists. is_halal set to true for the soy sauce variant; the oyster sauce variant is also generally considered halal. Allergen note: oyster sauce contains shellfish; soy sauce contains soy and may contain gluten.
